title: Overcoming catastrophic forgetting in neural networks
summary: Introduces Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC), which constrains updates on parameters with high Fisher importance to reduce forgetting in sequential learning. It matters because it formalizes the stability-plasticity trade-off as a parameter-space regularization objective.
assumptions: Diagonal Fisher approximation is sufficient.
Previous-task optima are encoded in θ* and F.
contributions: EWC regularizer based on Fisher information.
Sequential training results on Atari and supervised tasks.
claims: Penalizing changes to important parameters mitigates catastrophic forgetting.
conclusions: EWC improves retention versus naive fine-tuning in several sequential settings.
limitations: Requires storing per-parameter importance.
Diagonal approximation can miss parameter interactions.
future_work: Better posterior approximations.
Task-free variants without explicit task boundaries.

title: Continual Learning Through Synaptic Intelligence
summary: Proposes Synaptic Intelligence (SI), an online path-integral measure of parameter importance that regularizes future updates. It matters for low-overhead continual regularization without needing second-order matrices.
assumptions: Local gradient-path statistics reflect parameter importance.
Task transitions can be detected to consolidate importance.
claims: SI attains forgetting reduction with lower compute than EWC-like estimates.
conclusions: Path-integral importance is a practical CL regularizer.
limitations: Still depends on consolidation points.
May underperform with heavy distribution drift.
future_work: Boundary-free continual consolidation.

title: Gradient Episodic Memory for Continual Learning
summary: Introduces GEM, projecting gradients to avoid increasing loss on episodic memories from past tasks. It matters as a direct constraint-based method balancing adaptation and retention.
assumptions: Stored episodic samples represent prior tasks sufficiently.
Task identity exists during training for memory partitioning.
claims: Projected gradients reduce forgetting while permitting transfer.
conclusions: Memory-constrained optimization is effective but costly.
limitations: QP projection overhead.
Relies on episodic memory and often task structure.
future_work: Cheaper approximations and task-agnostic memory.

title: Efficient Lifelong Learning with A-GEM
summary: A-GEM replaces GEM’s per-task constraints with a single reference gradient from memory for cheaper projection. It matters because it scales constrained replay to larger models.
assumptions: Single reference gradient is a good proxy for past-task constraints.
contributions: Benchmarks showing runtime gains over GEM.
claims: Comparable forgetting control with improved efficiency.
conclusions: Approximate projection improves practicality of replay constraints.
limitations: Proxy constraint can miss per-task violations.
future_work: Adaptive reference sampling and task-free memory.

title: Learning without Forgetting
summary: Uses distillation on old outputs while learning new tasks to preserve prior behavior without old data. It matters as a foundational regularization baseline for CL.
assumptions: Teacher outputs from previous model approximate old-task function.
claims: Old-task performance can be retained without old data storage.
conclusions: Distillation mitigates forgetting in incremental settings.
limitations: Can drift when old logits are poorly calibrated.
future_work: Combine with replay and representation constraints.

title: iCaRL: Incremental Classifier and Representation Learning
summary: Combines rehearsal exemplars, representation learning, and nearest-mean classification for class-incremental learning. It matters as a canonical replay baseline in CL vision.
assumptions: Small exemplar sets approximate old class distributions.
claims: Exemplar rehearsal plus distillation improves incremental accuracy.
conclusions: Replay is powerful in class-incremental settings.
limitations: Memory selection bias and storage overhead.
future_work: Task-free sampling and stronger representation invariance.

title: Progressive Neural Networks
summary: Adds new network columns for new tasks with lateral reuse from frozen previous columns. It matters for strong retention via architectural isolation.
assumptions: Task sequence allows column growth.
Memory growth is acceptable.
claims: Frozen prior columns prevent forgetting.
conclusions: Strong retention at high parameter cost.
limitations: Linear growth in model size.
Requires task boundaries.
future_work: Parameter-efficient adapters and shared activations.

title: PackNet: Adding Multiple Tasks to a Single Network by Iterative Pruning
summary: Uses iterative pruning/freezing to allocate subnetworks per task within one backbone. It matters as a strong sparse-allocation CL strategy.
assumptions: Task boundaries are known.
Per-task masks can be stored.
claims: Multiple tasks can be packed with minimal interference.
conclusions: Sparse subnetworks retain old tasks effectively.
limitations: Task-ID/mask dependence conflicts with task-agnostic goals.
future_work: Continuous, task-free mask dynamics.

title: Overcoming catastrophic forgetting with hard attention to the task
summary: HAT learns task-conditioned gates to isolate task-relevant parameters. It matters as a masking baseline showing strong retention but explicit task dependence.
assumptions: Task identity available at train/test.
Task embeddings can be maintained.
claims: Task-specific gating protects old knowledge.
conclusions: Effective retention with explicit task signals.
limitations: Not task-agnostic.
Gate saturation sensitivity.
future_work: Boundary-free gating signals from data dynamics.

title: Alleviating catastrophic forgetting using context-dependent gating and synaptic stabilization
summary: Combines context-dependent sparse gating with synaptic stabilization (e.g., SI/EWC style) to reduce interference. It matters as a hybrid approach balancing isolation and sharing.
assumptions: Context signals available for gating.
Importance estimates remain meaningful over tasks.
claims: Hybrid strategy outperforms either component alone in many settings.
conclusions: Sparse contextual activation can reduce interference.
limitations: Requires context mechanism; not fully task-free.
future_work: Self-generated context from activation statistics.

title: Piggyback: Adapting a Single Network to Multiple Tasks by Learning to Mask Weights
summary: Learns binary masks over fixed backbone weights to add tasks without changing shared parameters. It matters as low-overhead task-specialization baseline.
assumptions: Task-specific masks are stored and selected.
claims: Competitive transfer with tiny per-task overhead.
conclusions: Masking can avoid interference when task IDs are available.
limitations: Depends on mask storage/task routing.
future_work: Continuous mask dynamics without explicit task identifiers.

title: Task Agnostic Continual Learning via Meta Learning
summary: Targets continual learning without task boundaries by meta-learning update behavior from online signals. It matters because task-agnosticity aligns with the requested constraints.
assumptions: Meta-objective captures long-horizon retention/adaptation trade-offs.
claims: Can operate without explicit task IDs.
conclusions: Meta-learned update rules improve task-free adaptation.
limitations: Meta-training complexity and sensitivity.
future_work: Scalable meta-objectives for deep continual models.

title: Online Continual Learning with Maximally Interfered Retrieval
summary: MIR selects replay samples most likely to interfere with current updates, improving memory efficiency. It matters for online CL where storage and compute are constrained.
assumptions: Interference score approximates future forgetting risk.
claims: Better online CL than random replay under equal memory.
conclusions: Prioritizing interfering samples strengthens retention.
limitations: Extra scoring overhead per step.
future_work: Approximate retrieval for larger memory banks.

title: Dark Experience for General Continual Learning: a Strong, Simple Baseline
summary: Introduces DER/DER++ using logit replay (dark knowledge) plus supervised replay, yielding strong simple baselines. It matters because many newer CL methods compare against DER++.
assumptions: Stored logits preserve informative targets over time.
claims: DER++ is hard to beat across common CL benchmarks.
conclusions: Strong baselines are critical for fair CL evaluation.
limitations: Replay memory footprint and privacy constraints.
future_work: Task-free memory compression and adaptive replay.

title: Supermasks in Superposition
summary: Represents multiple tasks in a single weight tensor via task-specific binary supermasks. It matters as a high-capacity masking baseline and a direct comparator to mask-free activation adaptation.
assumptions: Task-specific mask selection available.
Shared random weights can support many masked subnetworks.
claims: Many tasks can coexist with minimal interference via masks.
conclusions: Masking provides strong retention but uses task routing.
limitations: Explicit masks/task IDs conflict with task-agnostic requirement.
future_work: Continuous and task-free masking/activation control.

title: Avalanche: an End-to-End Library for Continual Learning
summary: Presents a unified toolkit for CL scenarios, strategies, metrics, and benchmarks. It matters for reproducible evaluation and fast ablation of activation-function proposals.
assumptions: Standardized APIs improve experimental comparability.
claims: Reduces engineering friction and evaluation inconsistency.
conclusions: Framework support accelerates CL research cycles.
limitations: Coverage depends on implemented plugins/scenarios.
future_work: Broader task-free/online RL continual scenarios.

title: Continual evaluation for lifelong learning: Identifying the stability gap
summary: Argues conventional evaluation can hide instability and proposes continual evaluation diagnostics. It matters for assessing online adaptive activations beyond final accuracy.
assumptions: Evaluation protocol materially changes method ranking.
contributions: Stability-gap framing and continual evaluation recommendations.
claims: Static evaluation snapshots can overestimate robustness.
conclusions: Temporal diagnostics are necessary in lifelong settings.
limitations: Protocol adoption is not yet universal.
future_work: Standardized continual-evaluation leaderboards.

title: A Comprehensive Survey of Continual Learning: Theory, Method and Application
summary: Large-scale survey covering CL settings, methods, theory, and applications. It matters for positioning activation-centric CL among replay, regularization, and architecture families.
assumptions: Existing CL taxonomy can organize fast-evolving methods.
claims: No single method dominates across all settings.
conclusions: Task-free and scalable CL remain open challenges.
limitations: Survey lag versus rapidly appearing methods.
future_work: Unified protocols and realistic continual streams.

title: Learnability and Algorithm for Continual Learning
summary: Provides theoretical framing for CL learnability and algorithmic implications. It matters for grounding activation-update rules in formal conditions.
assumptions: Sequence distributions follow defined shift model.
Hypothesis class supports derived generalization bounds.
contributions: Formal CL learnability definitions and bounds.
claims: Certain replay/regularization schemes have provable advantages under shift assumptions.
conclusions: Theoretical tools can guide practical CL design.
limitations: Theory relies on simplified assumptions relative to deep practice.
future_work: Bridge bounds with large-scale empirical deep CL.

title: Mining Generalizable Activation Functions
summary: Explores automated discovery of activation functions with stronger cross-task generalization. It matters directly for designing adaptive nonlinearities for continual learning.
assumptions: Search space of compositional nonlinearities contains transferable candidates.
claims: Discovered activations can outperform fixed hand-designed functions.
conclusions: Automated activation design is promising for adaptability.
limitations: Search cost and reproducibility sensitivity.
future_work: Online/task-free activation adaptation during continual streams.

title: Gaussian Error Linear Units (GELUs)
summary: Defines GELU, a smooth nonlinearity weighting inputs by value via Gaussian CDF. It matters as a baseline non-saturating activation with stable gradients.
assumptions: Smooth stochastic-like gating improves optimization/generalization.
claims: GELU often outperforms ReLU/ELU on modern architectures.
conclusions: Value-weighted smooth gating is effective.
limitations: No continual-adaptation mechanism built in.
future_work: Dynamic parameterized GELU variants.

title: Self-Normalizing Neural Networks
summary: Introduces SELU and fixed-point analysis showing mean/variance contraction toward stable values. It matters for the self-stabilization requirement in deep continual networks.
assumptions: Inputs satisfy independence/finite variance approximations.
Network architecture conditions for contraction hold.
claims: Layer statistics converge to stable region without batch norm.
conclusions: Activation design can enforce statistical stability.
limitations: Assumptions can weaken under residual/attention architectures.
future_work: Self-normalizing activations for modern deep architectures and CL.

title: Searching for Activation Functions
summary: Discovers Swish via automatic search and reports improved performance over ReLU in several settings. It matters as evidence that activation search can outperform fixed handcrafted choices.
assumptions: Search spaces include transferable nonlinear forms.
claims: Swish improves accuracy on multiple benchmarks.
conclusions: Automated discovery is effective for activation design.
limitations: Search can be computationally expensive.
future_work: Task-aware or continual activation search.

title: Mish: A Self Regularized Non-Monotonic Activation Function
summary: Introduces Mish activation with smooth non-monotonic behavior and empirical gains across tasks. It matters as a non-saturating baseline with richer curvature than ReLU.
assumptions: Smooth non-monotonicity helps optimization landscapes.
claims: Competitive or improved performance vs ReLU/Swish in many settings.
conclusions: Curved smooth activations can help representation quality.
limitations: No explicit continual-learning mechanism.
future_work: Adaptive Mish-like parameterizations for nonstationary streams.

title: Deep Learning with S-shaped Rectified Linear Activation Units
summary: Proposes SReLU, a learnable piecewise-linear activation with left/right slopes and thresholds. It matters for lightweight parameterized activations relevant to dynamic CL designs.
assumptions: Per-channel activation parameters can be trained stably.
contributions: Learnable piecewise activation with few parameters.
claims: SReLU outperforms common fixed activations in tested CNNs.
conclusions: Low-parameter adaptive nonlinearities are effective.
limitations: Not designed for explicit continual adaptation.
future_work: Online adaptation of piecewise parameters under shift.

title: Dynamic ReLU
summary: Conditions ReLU parameters on input context, creating dynamic piecewise-linear activations. It matters as a direct precursor to online adaptive activation in continual settings.
assumptions: Input-conditioned coefficients can be predicted with low overhead.
claims: Dynamic activations improve representational capacity and accuracy.
conclusions: Activation adaptivity can deliver gains with modest overhead.
limitations: Conditioning modules add complexity; CL behavior not directly studied.
future_work: Continual-learning-aware dynamic activation schedules.

title: Activate or Not: Learning Customized Activation
summary: Introduces ACON family and meta-ACON gating, where activation shape is learnable and data-dependent. It matters for designing controllable plasticity in nonlinearities.
assumptions: Learnable activation coefficients can be optimized robustly.
claims: ACON variants yield consistent gains in vision backbones.
conclusions: Learning activation shape is practical and beneficial.
limitations: No explicit continual forgetting analysis.
future_work: Task-free online adaptation of activation parameters.

title: Continual World: A Robotic Benchmark for Continual Reinforcement Learning
summary: Introduces the Continual World benchmark (CW10/CW20) for continual RL using MetaWorld tasks, with baselines and reproducible scripts. It matters as a mandatory dataset/benchmark source for evaluating activation-based CL in RL.
assumptions: Sequential robotic task suites model realistic nonstationary RL.
contributions: Standardized continual RL benchmark and code.
claims: Existing CL methods struggle on long-horizon robotic sequences.
conclusions: Benchmark reveals significant stability-plasticity challenges.
limitations: Primarily MuJoCo/MetaWorld scope; transfer to other domains is open.
future_work: Broader domains and stronger task-agnostic baselines.

title: A Hierarchically Structured Task-Agnostic Continual Learning Algorithm
summary: Presents a hierarchical task-agnostic CL algorithm that adapts structure without explicit task IDs. It matters directly for the user’s no-task-identity constraint.
assumptions: Hierarchical structure can capture recurring subproblems across streams.
claims: Improves retention and transfer without explicit task labels.
conclusions: Structured latent organization supports task-free continual adaptation.
limitations: Implementation complexity and sensitivity to hierarchy design.
future_work: Scaling to multimodal and larger architectures.

title: Continual Learning in Real World Applications: A Review
summary: Reviews deployment-oriented CL challenges across application domains. It matters for aligning activation-function proposals with practical constraints (compute, memory, robustness).
assumptions: Application constraints significantly shape viable CL methods.
claims: Real-world CL requires stronger robustness and efficiency guarantees.
conclusions: Bridging benchmark and deployment gaps is a central CL priority.
limitations: Review breadth limits methodological depth per subdomain.
future_work: Standardized realistic continual benchmarks and protocols.

title: Continual World GitHub Repository
summary: Reference implementation and scripts for continual RL experiments (single-task, continual, multi-task), with reproducibility scripts and benchmark setup details.
assumptions: MuJoCo and MetaWorld dependencies are available.
Task sequences like CW10/CW20 represent CL stress tests.
contributions: Executable benchmark code and experiment scripts.
claims: Provides reproducible protocol for continual RL baselines.
conclusions: Useful codebase for evaluating adaptive activation methods in RL streams.
limitations: No explicit OSS license file in repository root.
future_work: Update dependencies and expand benchmark scenarios.

title: Avalanche GitHub Repository
summary: Widely used CL library offering benchmark streams, methods, metrics, and logging utilities for reproducible experiments.
assumptions: Modular strategy interfaces can standardize CL experiments.
claims: Enables consistent evaluation across CL methods.
conclusions: Good backbone for activation-function ablations.
limitations: Coverage depends on available plugins and scenario implementations.
future_work: Expanded support for online/task-free RL CL.

title: Meta-World: A Benchmark and Evaluation for Multi-Task and Meta Reinforcement Learning
summary: Defines Meta-World robotic task suite used by Continual World. It matters because benchmark assumptions inherit from this environment and task distribution.
assumptions: Benchmark tasks reflect transfer and adaptation structure in manipulation.
claims: Provides rigorous evaluation framework for transfer-capable RL methods.
conclusions: Useful basis for continual RL streams.
limitations: Simulation-to-real gap.
future_work: Harder real-world and long-horizon benchmark variants.

title: Permuted MNIST Benchmark Setup
authors: Goodfellow et al. benchmark usage in CL literature
summary: Standard synthetic continual-learning benchmark creating sequential tasks by pixel permutation of MNIST. It matters for rapid controlled forgetting diagnostics.
assumptions: Permutation-induced task changes model nonstationarity.
contributions: Low-cost, widely used CL benchmark protocol.
claims: Useful for controlled forgetting comparisons.
conclusions: Common first-pass benchmark but limited realism.
limitations: Unrealistic task semantics and low complexity.
future_work: Use alongside realistic streams (CIFAR/Omniglot/RL).

title: Split CIFAR Benchmark Setup
summary: Class-incremental benchmark splitting CIFAR classes across sequential stages. It matters for measuring representation drift and forgetting in vision streams.
assumptions: Class splits approximate incremental category learning.
claims: Exposes severe forgetting in naive fine-tuning.
conclusions: Essential baseline benchmark for activation CL proposals.
limitations: Limited domain diversity.
future_work: Broader domain-shift and compositional streams.

title: A Survey on Knowledge Distillation of Large Language Models
summary: Surveys distillation mechanisms relevant to preserving prior behaviors under model updates; useful as auxiliary perspective for continual retention losses.
assumptions: Teacher outputs encode transferable prior knowledge.
contributions: Taxonomy of distillation objectives and settings.
claims: Distillation remains a core retention mechanism across scales.
conclusions: Distillation design choices strongly affect stability and transfer.
limitations: Focus is LLM-centric rather than continual-learning benchmarks.
future_work: Online distillation in nonstationary continual streams.

title: First-Order Meta-Learning for Continual Learning
summary: Studies first-order meta-learning strategies for continual adaptation with reduced computational overhead.
assumptions: First-order approximations can capture useful meta-gradients for CL.
claims: First-order methods can remain competitive for continual adaptation.
conclusions: Meta-learning remains viable when efficiency constraints are tight.
limitations: Performance may lag second-order variants in some regimes.
future_work: Task-agnostic online meta-learning with dynamic activations.

title: Addressing Loss of Plasticity and Catastrophic Forgetting in Continual Learning
summary: Introduces Utility-based Perturbed Gradient Descent (UPGD) to jointly address catastrophic forgetting and loss of plasticity under unknown task boundaries.
assumptions: Unit utility estimates can separate stable from underused units.
contributions: UPGD optimizer for continual representation learning in streaming settings.
claims: UPGD improves retention and continued adaptation in supervised and RL continual setups.
conclusions: Balancing protection of useful units with rejuvenation of less useful units mitigates both forgetting and plasticity loss.
limitations: Utility estimation quality can affect optimizer behavior.
future_work: Extend perturbation-control schedules to larger multimodal continual settings.

title: Analyzing and Reducing Catastrophic Forgetting in Parameter Efficient Tuning
summary: Analyzes mode connectivity in continual LLM fine-tuning and proposes Interpolation-based LoRA (I-LoRA) with strong gains on domain-incremental benchmarks.
assumptions: Low-loss valleys connect adjacent continual minima in PEFT space.
contributions: Mode-connectivity analysis for CL with LLM PEFT and I-LoRA method.
claims: I-LoRA improves stability-plasticity trade-off and outperforms prior baselines.
conclusions: Interpolation in LoRA parameter space is effective for continual adaptation.
limitations: Focused on domain-specific LLM continual fine-tuning scenarios.
future_work: Generalize interpolation memory to broader task-free continual setups.
